questions.question: Pushed into an extremely high radiation field, a GM survey meter without anti-saturation circuitry can read near zero. What causes this failure?
options: The energy-compensating sleeve blocks low-energy scatter, which makes up most of the field close to a source
Events arrive faster than the tube's dead time allows it to recover, so pulses pile into a continuous blur the circuit cannot count and the reading folds back toward zero
The strong field drops the tube into the ionization region, where the chamber is too insensitive to deflect the needle
The halogen quench gas is consumed within seconds, leaving the tube unable to produce any pulses
questions.explanation: Every GM pulse is followed by roughly 100 microseconds of dead time. In an extreme field, events arrive faster than the tube can reset, pulses merge into a continuous discharge, and the displayed reading collapses — saturation, or fold-back. That is why you switch the meter on and verify response before approaching, and treat a climb-then-collapse reading as a signal to retreat immediately.

questions.question: A boundary must be set and a stay time calculated near an exposure device. Why is the ionization chamber the instrument of record for those numbers?
options: Its avalanche guarantees that every single event registers, so no dose is missed
Its NaI(Tl) crystal and photomultiplier give it the greatest sensitivity of any field instrument
It collects only the primary ion pairs, so its current is proportional to true exposure rate, its energy response is flat, and it does not fold back at radiography dose rates
It is the only instrument that responds to both photons and neutrons
questions.explanation: Operating in the ionization (saturation) region, the chamber adds no gas amplification: the tiny electrometer current is directly proportional to the energy deposited. That buys accuracy in strong fields with a flat energy response — exactly what you need when the number itself drives a boundary or stay-time decision. Avalanches belong to GM tubes and crystals to scintillators, both search instruments.

questions.question: Inside a neutron rem meter, what is the role of the boron-10 or helium-3 in the central proportional tube?
options: To capture slow neutrons and convert each capture into energetic charged particles — an alpha plus lithium-7, or a proton — that ionize the tube's gas
To slow incoming fast neutrons down to thermal speeds before they are counted
To absorb gamma photons so that only neutron events can reach the detector
To scintillate, converting neutron energy into light flashes for a photomultiplier
questions.explanation: Neutrons carry no charge and cannot ionize gas directly, so detection runs through capture reactions: boron-10 splits into an energetic alpha plus lithium-7, and helium-3 emits a proton. Those charged particles ionize the gas. Slowing the neutrons is the polyethylene moderator's job, and gamma rejection is done electronically by pulse-height discrimination — not by absorption.

questions.question: Under 10 CFR 34.25, an instrument used to make radiographic surveys must be capable of measuring a range of:
options: 0.1 mrem per hour through 50 mrem per hour
2 mrem per hour through 100 mrem per hour
1 rem per hour through 10 rem per hour
2 mrem per hour through 1 rem per hour
questions.explanation: The required span is 2 mrem/h through 1 rem/h (0.02 mSv/h through 0.01 Sv/h). The low end covers boundary and posting decisions; the high end covers readings near the device when something has gone wrong. An instrument that cannot cover that whole range is not a radiography survey meter, whatever its other virtues.

questions.question: Approaching the device after an exposure, your analog meter's needle settles on 1.4 on the 0–2 mR/h scale with the range selector at ×100. The dose rate at your position is:
options: 1.4 mR/h
14 mR/h
140 mR/h
questions.explanation: An analog reading is always needle times range multiplier: 1.4 × 100 = 140 mR/h. Reporting the needle alone would understate the field one hundred times over — the classic survey-meter error, and always in the dangerous direction when the range switch is high.

questions.question: Which of the following does NOT require a radiography survey meter to be recalibrated before it is used again?
options: Replacing its batteries during a routine pre-use check
Repair of the range selector switch by the instrument shop
Passing the 6-month anniversary of its last calibration
Replacement of the detector tube
questions.explanation: 10 CFR 34.25(b) requires calibration at intervals not to exceed 6 months and after each instrument servicing — with battery changes named as the single exception. After a battery change you still re-run the battery check and confirm the meter responds to a known field, but no recalibration is triggered. Any other servicing leaves accuracy unverified until the instrument is recalibrated.

questions.question: Which statement correctly gives the defining threshold of a very high radiation area?
options: An area where an individual could receive more than 100 mrem in 1 hour at 30 cm
An area where the absorbed dose could exceed 500 rad (5 Gy) in 1 hour at 1 meter
An area where an individual could receive more than 500 mrem dose equivalent in 1 hour at 30 cm
Any area reading more than 5 mrem in 1 hour at 30 cm from the source
questions.explanation: The very-high-radiation-area threshold changes quantity, unit, and distance all at once: 500 rad absorbed dose in 1 hour measured at 1 meter — not a dose equivalent at 30 cm. The 100 mrem figure defines a high radiation area and 5 mrem a radiation area, both in 1 hour at 30 cm. The required posting is GRAVE DANGER, VERY HIGH RADIATION AREA.

questions.question: During each hour of operation the source is exposed for a total of 15 minutes. A spot just outside the restricted-area boundary reads 8 mR/h during exposures and background the rest of the time. Under the public dose limits of 10 CFR 20.1301, that spot is:
options: Out of compliance — no unrestricted-area location may ever read above 2 mR/h on the meter
Out of compliance — the public limit at the boundary is 100 mrem in any one hour
Acceptable only if the spot is posted CAUTION, RADIATION AREA
Within the limit — the rule caps dose received in any one hour at 2 mrem, and 8 mR/h for 15 minutes delivers 2 mrem
questions.explanation: The unrestricted-area limit is 2 mrem in any one hour — a dose, not an instantaneous dose rate. With the source out only 15 minutes per hour, 8 mR/h × 0.25 h = 2 mrem, which does not exceed the limit (100 mrem is the annual public limit). Many companies still rope off at the 2 mR/h-during-exposure line because it is conservative and simple to verify; your license and procedures state which approach you use.

questions.question: You crank the source in after a shot: the crank turned smoothly and the cable counter shows full retraction. Under 10 CFR 34.49, what must still happen before anyone exchanges film or dismantles equipment?
options: A survey with a calibrated, operable instrument covering the exposure device and the entire length of the guide tube, made while approaching them
Nothing further — smooth retraction plus the device's source-position indicator verifies shielding
A short wait for residual radiation in the area to die down before walking in
A single reading taken from the crank position to confirm the field has dropped
questions.explanation: A drive cable can disconnect and a source can hang up in the tube while the crank feels normal and the indicators look right. 10 CFR 34.49 requires a post-exposure survey of the device and guide tube — full circumference of the device and the whole tube out to the exposure head — proving the source is shielded before film, hands, or breakdown. There is no residual radiation to wait out: if the source is shielded the field is already down.

questions.question: Your RSO assigns a 30 mR dose budget for a task at a position where the survey meter reads 120 mR/h. The maximum stay time is:
options: 4 minutes
15 minutes
25 minutes
2.5 hours
questions.explanation: Stay time = allowed dose ÷ measured dose rate = 30 mR ÷ 120 mR/h = 0.25 h = 15 minutes. The dose rate must be measured at the actual working position, a timekeeper enforces the limit, and any change in source, shielding, or position forces a re-survey and a fresh calculation. A stay time is a ceiling — ALARA means coming in under it.
